speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven. Dr. Max Epstein. March 10, 1995. OK, we kind of raced you from finding your mother to America. So let me clarify just a few things. You fought in the Independence Day War in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Independence, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I pointed out, the quest for normalcy-- the hunger for normalcy-- was so strong that anything, no matter if it was dangerous or not, it seemed to me that if I can become normal, then I would-- that I would-- that-- as I said, it was the catharsis for the-- for the whatever psychological consequences of the war may be, and I'm not immune to them anymore than anybody else. And so I felt that gave me an opportunity to move into normalcy. It was the end of World War II. It was not-- the Korean War wasn't in yet-- on yet, and we all lived under the war movies. And the normalcy, at least from an American's point of view, a GI is-- actually, a person in the Unite States at that time who was not a GI was, in effect, a not normal person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I felt that if I could participate in something like that-- of course, aware of the-- of the significance and enormity, historical significance of what was happening. We obviously were aware of that. And the-- but particularly, in an individual sense, I felt that that was my ticket to normalcy. OK. After you came to America, did people ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't-- no. At that time, there were so many of us, that you were-- you know. You know, once you have a lot of them. Today, you are taping me because we are in the extinct species. But, you know, at that time, there were just too damn many of us as far as some people felt. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, it's-- no-- it-- yeah, you were a refugee. You were in concentration camps or whatever. So there was a lot of them too. And, you know. So it's-- no, it wasn't-- it wasn't really a major topic of conversation. And again, in my quest for normalcy, I wasn't going to raise it anyway. [LAUGHS] I wasn't about to. How do you feel these experiences formulated your life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily, I would have to go back to that quest for normalcy and the desire to succeed and to prove that I-- that they have not damaged me. In other words, I have-- I have made a very-- a success of my life. I have achieved-- at least-- I don't know. Don't ask me in, you know, intimate questions, because obviously we all have our ups and downs. But if you ask me from-- from an external viewpoint, I have achieved what one would consider a successful life. Whether it was in sciences, in-- in terms of prestige, in terms of positions, in terms of, even, financially, that I have achieved it all, and certain financial independence. And that I have achieved all of these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ambition to be somebody-- see, the thing-- the terrible thing about war was that you were going to die. When you were herded into the gas chambers, herded into these places, you were going to die without a trace. And if you take the three pictures of my children, this is the trace, and I've proven it. So I didn't die without a trace. And that's number one. Number two, they have made me-- you know-- you know what happens to people who are deprived of things? They look terrible. They act terrible. They become like animals, so then the oppressor turns around and says, look at the way you look. Look at the way you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- so, in my opinion, what they have done to us is not only did they have physically killed us and maimed us, but they have actually denigrated us. They have to tried to demoralize us. And I sort of decided, at least the conclusion was that I want to be normal. I'm going to succeed. I am to prove to these bastards that I am somebody, and that I can make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think this was primarily my entire probably-- I think this probably characterized or [INAUDIBLE] my behavior since then. Now, was I exaggerating at times? Probably. There's no question about it that, you know, I may have considered it too important. When I look back now, at the age of 70, and look back, you know, I can afford to say well, you know, it wasn't really necessary.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, this is like anybody who is a rich and says, you know, there's one thing you cannot buy when you are from money is poverty. You know, I cannot-- after I'm successful, I cannot buy failure. So it's very easy to say, well, you shouldn't have done this and that. You could have measured it better and wasted so much time and effort on this. You could have been [INAUDIBLE] set of priorities. But it's very easy to say, once you succeed, when you are fine-- I came to this country in the language of an American political. I ran scared. When you go-- you know, politicians, when they run and they want to win, they run scared. I run scared. And that's-- consequence was I didn't marry for 10 years after coming here. And my mother was frantic, needless to say. And-- but she-- she at least had enough time to see two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you do now. What your profession was, what your-- I know you have something else--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in the IIT Research Institute in IIT, primarily in defense systems. I got a PhD in electrical engineering, primarily with-- mostly in solid state physics. And then I moved on to-- and I stayed at IIT Research Institute until 1967. I then decided that I wanted to go to university, actually, as a faculty member, and I had two choices at the time. I was toying with the idea of going to Illinois. They were moving from the Navy Pier into the new campus. And then I got an offer from Northwestern University in '67. And everybody asked me why I go one to the other. I got identical offers and identical position, identical salary, except the parking was easier at Northwestern. If this tells you something about science and choice of jobs. [LAUGHTER] I hope this is not being shown to anybody in the administration of Northwest, or they will throw me out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Northwestern?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since March, 1967. That means that two weeks from now will be 28 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you taught that whole period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was moving from field to field. I was in magnetism, then I moved to optics, and I worked in biomedical engineering. Today, I have a position in both departments, in electrical engineering and biomedical engineering. I did most of my work in lasers in medicine and in endoscopy and holography. And I went into businesses, and I opened some stores, and I opened a company to make holograms. I dabbled in all kinds of things. I was very interested in seeing different walks of lives and different people. And I'm-- even though I lost a lot of money in that business-- obviously, since I'm not a businessmen-- but I have really been very thankful for the fact that I had an opportunity to meet people in different walks of life, in advertising, in art, in other things, which-- let me say that living at the University and trying to be a specialist and trying to succeed, you must become narrower and narrower, and therefore, you know, it's-- the ivory tower gets so narrow that it's about to collapse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever related your Holocaust experiences to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, books are around. They read them. They see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never in-- well, I would make a joke of something. You know, I would say, I mean-- I like you to be responsible, and the best way to do that is to build an Auschwitz and put you there. You know, I mean, you know-- if I make a statement like this, and they are very intelligent kids, they certainly know what I mean. And they can have a good laugh of it. They have a very good sense of humor, even better than mine, maybe. And-- and they have done it all by osmosis. You don't have to explain it. No, I was not relaying it. I was not relating it. I was not interested-- of course, both of my sons quit school here at the seventh grade. They finished their requirements in high school here, and they went to Israel and did their eight years there-- their last year there. One son, David, did one year in Jerusalem in economics, the third year. Michael is back there now, working. The connection, the belonging, the-- I have never had an identity problem, bless my father's heart. He gave me an identity, and there was no question about it. I think I gave my children an identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have no difficultly. I have no difficulty with Jews moaning in America-- am I a religion? Am I a nation? Am I this? Am I that? All of these questions are, in my opinion, irrelevant to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, by the way, I don't-- you know, I don't necessarily try to accuse them of not doing things right, because they do it within their framework and with their experiences. My experiences, my background as a child, as the war, as after the war, as everything else, gave me a very strong identity. Identity's a very important thing for a person to have, because that's what you really are. If you don't identify-- and that's the tragedy, probably, of modern times, even in America, is that you lack an identity. I have an identity. My kids inherited my identity without telling or preaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years after the war, what message or lesson do you have for your children, grandchildren, for the world, from your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- you know, to-- I'm very careful-- I'm going to refuse to answer that question on the grounds-- if you don't mind my legal language-- that it would be-- If I'm to be candid and truthful, I do not think that any experience, particularly of that length, can be summarized in any form without doing it injustice. Things are done by osmosis. They are done by being there, by-- it's like learning how to drive a car. You do not discuss driving a car. You drive it. You don't swim, you don't discuss-- it's-- it's in the stream of things. It's in the life of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is-- now what do you do-- how do you retain an ethical and moral life, which I think is important to transfer to your children? Have I learned morality and ethics in concentration camps? Probably not. I probably brought it with me from home. There was not a school for ethics or morality. Did I see some very highly moral and ethical people? Of course I did. Did I learn something from it? Yes. As I pointed out, it was very dark. I do remember the sparks. They were very bright. I want to concentrate on the sparks.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I do not want to be guilty of trying to put something in a content of a sentence or two and somehow or other-- even not-- not all encompassing, even-- even implying that you can even touch the truth by somehow summarizing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add that we have not covered?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of it. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not really. This is a very long session already, beyond what you planed to do originally. I think, again, just like I refused to summarize it in one or two sentences, I think it would be improper for me to say, well, if I just do a little bit more, then it will-- then it will finish it. It's an unfinished thing. First of all, let me just say something that has to do with everybody at the time, and that is you are always going to be asked to be a judge of your situation and the times, whether you want to live ethically. Always be challenged at it. And you do not-- you cannot say that, from now on, I have a pat answer to things, that I know how to live, that I know how to behave. You don't. You are challenged all the time. I had that experience at work, where there are always red lines, and you always see them receding, and you always-- you don't know-- first of all, you don't know what to draw in, but when you draw them, they do not remain permanent. It is-- there is-- there's no such a thing that from now on, live happily ever after. You don't have to worry about judgment. You judge every minute of the day. If that's the thing that I convey to my children, I hope that that would be probably all I can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. How about if we bring [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Is it? Are we on? Well, I wanted-- you wanted me to show the tattoo and just give you a historical background. Tattoos, for numbers. The series without a letter and series A were given on this side of the hand until summer, 1944. After summer 1944, they put it inside the hand with Series B. And mine is B7702. And of course, the seven is the European style with a line across it. And if you can see it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so this is a little history of the tattoos. And they were given only [INAUDIBLE] before in Auschwitz. No other camps have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you want-- this is a picture of my mother and myself. And I think that this is '27, so I must be two. No, August '27. I remember. That must be two and 1/2 years old. And this is in the house where we lived. This is the house my father built.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this taken for a special reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. My mother was obsessed with pictures. I mean, whether it was actresses or herself or anybody else, we had pictures. I regret that I didn't go back to Poland and find where she buried about four or five big boxes of pictures, which I never, you know, took it with me. But no, she always loved pictures. She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and I-- my mother's head is missing-- as-- on the main drag in Lodz. And I think that I am here-- I don't remember how old-- three or four or something. No, more than that. Five. I'm five years old. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in my mother and I at the resort place in 1931. And this is somewhere in the park, probably. There were a lot of parks that we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Checkochinick. ?] If you-- I challenge you to repeat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a cottage that we rented with my aunt. In the window, you see my uncle on the left, my aunt, and my mother to the right, my cousin, Lillian, and myself. I'm 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Aunt and uncle's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rizik was the family name. His name was Max, and her name was [? Lyuba. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I. I'm 13 years old. We are attending a wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is-- that is brief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war. Nonantola, Italy, where I found my mother and the friend. And those are the people who were there, and I am there, of course. I hope you can see me. And above it is one of my best friends who died here several years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the one that-- it's from the right. You're looking at the picture, the-- the-- next to the right, second from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Top row?

speaker: interviewer
text: Front row or back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Top row.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is 1946-- beginning of 1946-- in Kibbutz, Tel Yitzhak, in Israel. My mother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself and my mother. I'm in uniform, 1948, in Israeli army.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's brothers and sister-in-law. On the right, Abe, is the older one, my aunt, Rose, and on the left is Frank, her husband--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --My other uncle. Oklahoma City, 1953. Oh no, I beg your pardon. This is a picture we got from them when we were in Israel, and that was sometime at the end of the war. Sorry about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Oklahoma City, 1953. From left to right, my uncle Abe, my mother, and myself. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, from left to right, my mother-in-law Esther, my wife, Judith, and my mother, and-- at our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1963. September 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael. He's 30 years old today, March 10th. And he was born in 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's your oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is David, born in August, 1966. And this is a picture taken, probably, two-- several years ago. He looks the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Deborah, born in July, 1968. And her name is Deborah. My mother's name was Dola-- she was born-- which was Deborah. She was born four months after my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling. OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Max, do you want to introduce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my wife, Judith, on my left. And this is my daughter, Deborah, on my right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sons are-- one is in Israel, the older one. Michael is in Israel. And the younger one, David, is in Seattle. And we stay quite close and talk often. As Deborah pointed out, the further you are, the more often you talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deborah, any comments about your father? What do you think is special about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Everything is special. I don't know what to say. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Special-- of course, anybody can interpret it any way they want, and I hope that they will know what to do. It's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy, you have anything to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he's very special to me. [LAUGHTER] And I encouraged him to say his story, because I think it shouldn't get lost. And I did see the movie, and it really impressed me. And I thought he should tell more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we appreciate you letting us into your home. And Max, we appreciate your patience with us and for telling and sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
